findings.description: verify_checksum slurps the entire stored file into memory via f.read() before hashing instead of streaming with sha256.update() over fixed-size blocks; with MAX_FILE_MB=200 (and no enforced ordering guaranteeing file_size_ok runs first) this pins 200MB+ of RSS per concurrent verification, inviting memory pressure / OOM under load.

assemble loops range(total_chunks - 1), silently dropping the final chunk and producing a truncated file (primary defect is logic — defer to critic-logic); the perf-relevant consequence is that the truncated output fails verify_checksum downstream and forces wasteful full re-uploads/re-assembly.

assemble already fetched session via self._sessions.get(upload_id) then calls is_complete(upload_id), which redundantly re-does the same dict lookup; trivial micro-cost, mentioned only for completeness.

_cleanup_chunks does os.path.exists() then os.remove() — two stat/unlink syscalls per chunk plus a TOCTOU window; using os.remove in try/except (or unlink missing_ok) halves syscalls, but this is cold cleanup-path overhead, not hot.
